claims.claim_text: The module says data scientists spend 80% of their time on feature engineering.
claims.anchor_text: Data scientists spend 80% of their time on feature engineering, then those features sit in notebooks, unused by others.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Feature engineering and feature reuse consume a large share of practical ML work, and without shared tooling many useful features remain trapped in one-off notebooks.
claims.rationale: The exact 80% figure is presented as fact but no allowlisted primary source was found that directly supports this wording.

claims.claim_text: Feature stores centralize feature definitions, support reuse and discovery, and help keep training and serving aligned.
claims.rationale: Feast's quickstart explicitly describes a centralized catalog, feature discovery/reuse, and support for training-serving consistency.

claims.claim_text: The module calls Feast the leading open-source feature store.
claims.anchor_text: This module covers Feast, the leading open-source feature store, on Kubernetes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This module covers Feast, an open-source feature store, in a Kubernetes-oriented deployment context.
claims.rationale: The superlative leading is not backed by an authoritative benchmark or market source.

claims.claim_text: Feast supports point-in-time-correct historical retrieval and low-latency online serving to help reduce training-serving skew.
claims.span_hint: section: What You'll Be Able to Do
claims.rationale: The Feast repository overview directly describes point-in-time-correct training retrieval and low-latency online serving.

claims.claim_text: The Michelangelo/Zipline/Feathr paragraph makes specific historical and quantitative claims about company feature stores.
claims.anchor_text: > 💡 **Did You Know?** Uber built Michelangelo (their ML platform) in 2017 and discovered that 60% of engineer time was spent on features. They created a feature store, and that 60% dropped to 15%. Other companies noticed: Airbnb built Zipline, LinkedIn built Feathr, and the pattern became a standard. Feast emerged as the open-source solution that anyone could use.
claims.suggested_rewrite: > 💡 **Did You Know?** Teams building production ML systems often introduce feature stores to reduce duplicated feature work, improve feature sharing, and support both training and serving. Open-source tools now let smaller teams adopt similar patterns without building everything in-house.
claims.rationale: The paragraph bundles real company history with unsupported precise percentages and broad trend claims, so the false specifics should be removed rather than weakly cited.

claims.claim_text: Feast feature services group and version the features used by a model.
claims.span_hint: section: Defining Features | paragraph after Feature Services code block
claims.rationale: Feast's FAQ says each model version corresponds to a feature service, which directly supports the model-feature grouping claim.

claims.claim_text: The training-serving skew callout claims feature stores guarantee identical feature computation and implies many production incidents.
claims.span_hint: section: Key Concepts | paragraph after table
claims.anchor_text: > 💡 **Did You Know?** The training-serving skew problem was so common that it got its own name. Models trained on batch-computed features would fail in production where features were computed differently. Feature stores guarantee that the exact same feature computation runs in both places—solving a problem that caused countless production incidents.
claims.suggested_rewrite: > 💡 **Did You Know?** Training-serving skew happens when features are computed differently in model development and production. Feature stores help reduce that risk by centralizing feature definitions and supporting point-in-time-correct retrieval for training alongside online serving for inference.
claims.rationale: The central principle is real, but the wording overstates guarantees and adds uncited scale claims about incidents.

claims.claim_text: Feast automatically performs point-in-time-correct historical feature retrieval.
claims.span_hint: section: Feature Store Patterns | Point-in-Time Joins
claims.rationale: The offline store reference explicitly says `get_historical_features` performs a point-in-time-correct join.

claims.claim_text: The module ties on-demand features to a specific Feast version and claims only two prior options.
claims.span_hint: section: Feature Freshness | callout after diagram
claims.anchor_text: Before Feast 0.20, you had two choices: materialize everything (slow) or compute outside Feast (training-serving skew).
claims.suggested_rewrite: Without on-demand features, teams often either materialize more data than they want or compute transformations outside the feature store.
claims.rationale: The exact version boundary and exhaustive two-choice framing were not directly supported by an allowlisted primary source.

claims.claim_text: Feast on-demand transformations run during both historical retrieval and online serving.
claims.span_hint: section: Feature Freshness | callout after diagram
claims.rationale: Feast's FAQ directly states that on-demand transformations run for `get_historical_features` and `get_online_features`.

claims.claim_text: The war story gives exact training and production accuracy numbers for an unattributed incident.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Time-Traveling Feature
claims.anchor_text: *A fraud detection model had 99% accuracy in training but 60% in production. The team couldn't figure out why.*
claims.suggested_rewrite: *A fraud detection model can look excellent in training and then underperform badly in production when its training data includes leaked future information.*
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed anecdote with fake-precise outcome numbers, so the numbers should be removed.

claims.claim_text: The lesson implies that not using a feature store means future-data leakage is likely.
claims.span_hint: section: War Story: The Time-Traveling Feature | final lesson
claims.anchor_text: **Lesson**: Point-in-time correctness isn't optional. If you're not using a feature store for training data, you're probably leaking future information.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Lesson**: Point-in-time correctness isn't optional. If your training pipeline does not use point-in-time-correct feature retrieval, you risk leaking future information.
claims.rationale: The real issue is point-in-time correctness, not feature-store usage as an absolute prerequisite.

further_reading.title: Feast Quickstart
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers core Feast concepts including online and offline stores, training-serving skew, feature services, and on-demand transformations.

further_reading.title: Feast Offline Store Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Gives the clearest primary-source explanation of point-in-time-correct historical retrieval and materialization semantics.

notes: Shared section-pool entries S001-S003 were not applicable to this module's feature-store claims, so no pool sources were used. Local API cold-start failed (`api: FAILED to start — see logs/api.log`), so the section pool was read directly from the repository file.
